anchor: Voice agents are eating the front desk — dental case study

posts.body_preview:
Dental receptionist job listings in the GTA are down 38% YoY.

I know why. I built the thing that replaced them.

Here's the full teardown of the voice agent we shipped for a 4-chair practice in North York 👇

I rebuilt a dental front desk with a voice agent. Here's the P&L.

Before: 2 receptionists, $92K/yr loaded cost, 31% of after-hours calls missed.

After: 1 receptionist, 1 voice agent, $6K/yr in compute. 94% after-hours bookings captured. Six weeks in.

Dental receptionists are the new travel agents.

The job isn't 'going away.' It's already gone. Most practice owners just haven't run the math yet.
Hot take: if your SaaS still needs a 'book a demo' CTA in 2026, you're not selling software. You're selling a Zoom call with bad slides.

The AI agent stack we run at UpliftAI (April 2026 edition).

7 slides. No fluff. The tools, the costs, the 'why' for each layer. Swipe →

What job will AI agents eat next?

I'll start: dental front desk. Already happening. Reply with yours.
Ran catering for 240 people tonight using an AI agent for the entire intake flow. Zero dropped emails. First time in 4 years that's been true.

POV: the dental receptionist job just got automated.

60-second reel — phone rings, agent answers, books appointment, SMS confirmation fires. No human in the loop. Built this for a North York practice six weeks ago.
I replaced a dental receptionist with a voice agent — and it books 94% of after-hours calls. Full cost breakdown in 50 seconds. Link to the checklist in the pinned comment.
